reviewer: B — Adversarial Top AP Student (took the exam, scored a 5, knows where the lesson advice oversimplifies)
module: Module 12 — AP Exam Synthesis and Free-Response Strategy
summary: The advice is solid. Nothing here is actively wrong. Three minor flags from the perspective of a student trying to push from 4 to 5 — places where the lesson is slightly too generic. None are blockers.

findings.issue: Calling these 'the six patterns' is a slight oversell. In recent years, the AP exam has mixed patterns within a single FRQ — e.g., a particle motion problem that also tests function-defined-by-integral within the same prompt. The lesson should note that patterns are RECOGNITION tools, not separate problems. A strong student knows that one FRQ can be patterns 2+6 simultaneously.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add one sentence to the 05 narration: 'And one FRQ can mix two patterns — pattern recognition is your first move, not your only one.' OR leave alone — the current overview honestly serves the 90th-percentile student well enough without this nuance, and adding it might confuse the 75th-percentile target.

findings.issue: The 'do parts (a) and (b) of every problem first' tactic is good advice but is most useful for students who FREEZE on harder parts. A student who is comfortable should NOT context-switch six times — context-switching has a cost too. The lesson presents it as universal; it's actually a strategy for the middle-of-the-pack student. A top student who's pacing well should generally go in order. Minor nuance — not worth a revision for a 10-min lesson.
findings.fix_suggestion: Optional softening: 'If you're worried about time, do the easy parts of every problem first.' But current phrasing is fine for the broad audience.

findings.issue: The BECAUSE rule is correctly emphasized — this IS the single highest-leverage rubric trick. No notes. This is the best slide in the deck.

The path slide says 'go earn the five.' Some students who watch this will not be aiming for a 5 — they're aiming for a 3 to get college credit. Either change to 'Go earn your score' or accept the aspirational framing. I'd accept the aspirational version — it's a coach-speech module by design.
findings.fix_suggestion: No change required. Keep the coach tone.

findings.issue: The pause example uses f given verbally, not graphically. This is unusual for the AP exam — the real test almost always gives f as a graph. Reasonable adaptation for a video format (can't show a complex graph mid-pause), but the student should know the real exam version uses a graph. The 10_silence walkthrough handles the math correctly.

recommendation: SHIP. The minor flags are nuance for ambitious students, not corrections. The lesson hits the broad target well.
